fed_monetary: The FOMC convenes April 29–30 with market pricing reflecting a 100% probability of a third consecutive hold at 3.50–3.75%, as policymakers navigate a stagflationary squeeze. CPI running at 3.3% YoY, core PCE at 2.97%, and PPI at a striking 6.0%—all above consensus—leave no room for easing, while the 10-year yield at 4.35% and WTI crude at $105 reinforce financial conditions that are already restrictive. The balance sheet (WALCL $6.71T) continues passive runoff; with the RRP facility at near-zero ($0.6B) and reserves at $2.9T, systemic liquidity remains adequate but the composite liquidity score sits at just the 31st percentile, underscoring tightening at the margin.
labor_data: The labor market is bifurcated: March NFP printed +178K against a +100K consensus—a substantial beat—yet unemployment held steady at 4.3% in line with estimates, and the University of Michigan sentiment index slipped to 53.3, missing the 55.5 estimate and signaling growing household unease. Weekly initial claims at 214K are broadly stable, but elevated PPI and WTI above $105 compress real wages and threaten to erode the consumer spending resilience that has kept payrolls firm. The labor market is currently the primary argument against near-term Fed easing; any softening in upcoming claims or JOLTS data would materially shift the rate-cut calculus.
fiscal_supply: The Treasury General Account stands at $1.006T, providing near-term fiscal runway, but the structural deficit trajectory and tariff-driven revenue uncertainty keep gross issuance elevated into the May quarterly refunding. With the 10-year at 4.35% and G4 central bank balance sheets at $25.3T in aggregate, incremental Treasury supply faces a less accommodative demand backdrop than in prior issuance cycles. The May 7 Quarterly Refunding Announcement is the critical near-term event for duration supply; any upward revision to coupon issuance sizes would pressure the long end and widen term premium.
corporate: High-yield OAS at 285 bps and IG OAS at 102 bps reflect contained but watchful credit conditions—spreads have not blown out despite stagflationary data, suggesting markets are still pricing a soft-landing tail. However, WTI crude at $105.13 (+5.8% in the session) compresses margins for energy-intensive industrials and transportation, and Cleveland Fed 1-year inflation expectations at 3.26% threaten further consumer pullback. Q1 earnings season must now reconcile the strong NFP print with the sentiment deterioration and tariff-driven cost-push pressures; guidance revisions to the downside remain the primary corporate risk.
next_catalyst: The BEA's Q1 2026 Advance GDP Estimate, due April 30, is the single most consequential near-term release: the Atlanta Fed GDPNow tracker pointed to just 1.2% SAAR growth as of April 21, and a sub-1% print—or outright contraction—would force a sharp repricing of the Fed's 'higher-for-longer' tolerance and accelerate rate-cut expectations despite above-target inflation.
sources: Fed expected to hold rates for third straight meeting at 3.5%–3.75% amid sticky inflation — CBS News, April 28 2026
Fed meeting preview April 2026: officials face stagflation risk with inflation above target and slowing growth — CNBC, April 28 2026
GDPNow Q1 2026 estimate revised down to 1.2% SAAR as of April 21 — Atlanta Fed
Q1 2026 Advance GDP Estimate scheduled for April 30, 2026 — Polymarket / BEA
Federal Reserve maintains federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% — Federal Reserve Board, March 18 2026 FOMC Statement
